summary:
The Italian Wars (1494–1559 AD) were a series of interconnected conflicts fought primarily on Italian soil between France, Spain, the Holy Roman Empire, various Italian states, the Papacy, and the Ottoman Empire — the first truly 'European' wars in the sense that they involved most of the continent's major powers competing over a shared prize, and the crucible in which the modern European state system was forged. The wars began when Charles VIII of France invaded Italy in 1494 to claim the Kingdom of Naples, triggering a struggle for Italian domination that lasted 65 years and ended with Spain's [[event:2496|Habsburg Empire]] in effective control of much of the peninsula. Italy, the wealthiest and most culturally advanced region of Europe, became the arena in which the new 'gunpowder empires' of Spain and France tested their emerging military technologies and administrative systems.

The Italian Wars had profound and lasting consequences. Militarily, they drove the development of the trace italienne — the low, thick, angled fortification system designed to resist cannon fire — that transformed European warfare and made sieges the dominant form of military conflict for the next two centuries. The Sack of Rome (1527) by mutinous Habsburg-Spanish troops traumatized the Papacy, accelerated the end of the Renaissance papacy's worldly ambitions, and scattered the humanist scholars and artists of Rome's cultural golden age. The Peace of Cateau-Cambrésis (1559), which ended the wars, confirmed Spanish dominance in Italy, ended French Italian ambitions, and established a pattern of Habsburg hegemony in European affairs that would persist until the [[event:2329|Peace of Westphalia]] (1648). The Italian Wars also indirectly contributed to the Protestant Reformation by demonstrating papal weakness and worldliness.
